title: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
tldr: The act of pulling a fact out of your head — not putting it back in — is what makes it stick. This is the study that proves quizzing beats reviewing.

sections.body: Everyone knows the feeling of studying hard and remembering nothing a week later. Two psychologists at Washington University wanted to settle a basic argument: when you're trying to learn something, are you better off reading it again, or testing yourself on it? Most students assume re-reading. It feels productive — the words go back in smoothly, and you feel more confident each pass. The researchers suspected that confidence was a trap.

Students read short factual passages, the kind you'd find in a science magazine. Then the group was split. Some students re-studied the passage a second time. Others put the passage away and took a test — they tried to recall what it said, with no notes. Crucially, the two groups spent the same amount of time. Later, everyone took a final test on the material, either five minutes, two days, or a full week afterward.

On a test taken five minutes later, the re-readers actually did slightly better — recent re-reading gives you a short-lived boost. But after a week, it flipped hard. The students who had tested themselves remembered about 61% of the material; the re-readers remembered only about 40%. Same time spent, but the self-testers held on to half again as much. The effort of dragging the answer out of memory, even when it was hard, is what cemented it.

This finding — now called 'retrieval practice' or 'the testing effect' — quietly rewired how cognitive scientists think about memory. Memory isn't a bucket you fill; it's a muscle that strengthens when you make it work. The struggle to recall is not a sign of failed learning, it IS the learning. This single idea now powers spaced-repetition apps, medical-school curricula, and language tools used by hundreds of millions of people.

The self-testers in the study consistently predicted they'd do worse than the re-readers — right up until the results came in. The technique that works best is the one that feels worst while you're doing it. Re-reading feels fluent and reassuring; testing feels like exposing how much you don't know. That mismatch is exactly why most people study the less effective way.

terms.def: Deliberately recalling information from memory (e.g. a quiz) rather than reviewing it. The act of retrieval is what strengthens the memory.
The well-replicated finding that being tested on material improves long-term retention more than spending the same time restudying it.

takeaways: Close the book and try to recall — that struggle is the learning, not a sign you failed.
Feeling confident after re-reading is a poor signal; it fades within days.
This is literally why the QuizMe tab exists: it's the highest-leverage way to make a study stick.

quiz.q: In the study, who remembered more material a full week later?
choices: Students who tested themselves on it
Both groups remembered the same amount
quiz.why: After a week the self-testers held ~61% vs ~40% for re-readers — even though re-readers did slightly better on a test taken 5 minutes after studying.

choices: Recalling information strengthens memory more than restudying it
Tests being unfair measures of knowledge
The boost you get from re-reading right before an exam
quiz.why: The testing effect is the finding that retrieving information from memory improves long-term retention more than passively reviewing it.

quiz.q: Why do most people pick the less effective study method?
choices: Re-reading feels fluent and confident while testing feels like struggling
quiz.why: Self-testers predicted they'd do worse, because retrieval feels effortful and exposes gaps — while re-reading feels smooth and reassuring. The comfortable method is the weaker one.
